patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman. Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry all the time, and I keep gaining weight no matter what I do.” Early in the visit, volunteer: hungry all day even right after eating, rapid weight gain over 2 years, low energy/tiring easily, trouble controlling eating, shame. If asked about history/behavior, share: big appetite since childhood, never feel full, short stature, small hands/feet, learning struggles/need extra time, stress skin-picking, difficulty with routine changes, loud snoring and sometimes waking up gasping. Do not mention unless directly asked: floppy baby/feeding issues, low muscle strength, constipation, low sweating, high pain tolerance, very low libido and delayed/irregular puberty/periods. Resist sharing at first: hiding food/secret eating, taking others’ food, occasional self-induced vomiting, and past thoughts of self-harm—only disclose if asked gently and after rapport; deny current plan/intent. Mislead slightly by worrying episodes are “night seizures,” mention energy drinks. Express misconceptions: “Genetic conditions aren’t treatable, so specialists feel like wasted money,” and fear insurance/job/life insurance impacts. If met with empathy, you soften, answer more fully, and ask, “Is there anything that can actually help?”

acceptable_alternatives: Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

required_evidence: Clinical phenotype consistent with Prader-Willi-like syndrome (hyperphagia/food-seeking with obesity, developmental delay/intellectual disability or learning difficulties, behavioral dysregulation/compulsivity, hypogonadism or delayed puberty, short stature or history of growth issues)
Genetic confirmation of a pathogenic point mutation in a gene known to cause a Prader-Willi-like phenotype (document variant and inheritance if available)

Exclusion of classic Prader-Willi mechanisms when relevant (e.g., absence of 15q11-q13 deletion, maternal uniparental disomy 15, or imprinting defect on standard testing) OR documentation that a point mutation explains the phenotype
Assessment for metabolic complications common in PWS/PWS-like states (insulin resistance/diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver) using available labs

If nocturnal events are part of the presentation: characterization of episodes and evaluation (history +/- EEG/sleep study) to distinguish parasomnias/seizures; absence of convincing nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy features supports primary diagnosis

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Often normal to mildly elevated; may meet criteria for impaired fasting glucose or type 2 diabetes depending on obesity/insulin resistance
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be in prediabetes (5.7–6.4%) or diabetes (≥6.5%) range if metabolic disease present
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated consistent with insulin resistance (hyperinsulinemia)
expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: May be elevated (dyslipidemia) in obesity/metabolic syndrome
expected_lab_results.LDL cholesterol: May be elevated; atherogenic pattern possible
expected_lab_results.HDL cholesterol: Often low in insulin resistance/metabolic syndrome
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Often elevated in insulin resistance/metabolic syndrome
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be elevated consistent with nonalcoholic fatty liver disease in obesity

medications: Somatropin 0.24 mg/kg/week subcutaneous daily (dose individualized; start low and titrate based on IGF-1 and clinical response)
non_pharmacological: Structured food security plan (controlled access to food, consistent meal schedule, calorie-controlled balanced diet) with caregiver involvement
Multidisciplinary care: endocrinology, nutrition, behavioral health/psychology, and (as needed) genetics counseling
Regular physical activity plan tailored to ability; focus on daily routine to support weight management
Screen and manage comorbidities: sleep-disordered breathing, scoliosis, hypogonadism/puberty issues, behavioral/psychiatric symptoms

Metabolic risk management using available labs: if diabetes/prediabetes present, implement intensive lifestyle interventions and consider referral for pharmacotherapy; if dyslipidemia present, implement diet/exercise and assess need for lipid-lowering therapy per age/risk guidelines; if elevated ALT, evaluate for NAFLD and counsel on weight loss
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 6-12 weeks after initiating/adjusting growth hormone and lifestyle plan; repeat metabolic labs in 3 months, then every 6-12 months depending on abnormalities

must_include: The condition resembles Prader-Willi but is caused by a specific point mutation (genetic explanation and what it means for prognosis)
Key expected features and complications (hyperphagia/weight gain risk, behavioral challenges, endocrine/metabolic risks)
Why alternative diagnoses (classic Prader-Willi mechanisms; nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy if relevant) are less likely or how they will be evaluated
Rationale for growth hormone therapy (body composition, growth, function) and that dosing/monitoring is individualized
Need for ongoing monitoring (IGF-1, growth parameters, metabolic labs, sleep-related symptoms) and when to seek care
Multidisciplinary approach and caregiver role in implementing the plan
Discuss potential adverse effects and warning signs relevant to growth hormone (e.g., headaches/visual changes, edema, worsening sleep-breathing symptoms) and need to report promptly
Review allergies (amoxicillin, latex) and how they affect care (avoidance and documentation)
Discuss genetic implications for family (inheritance/recurrence risk if known; offer genetics counseling)
Food security strategies (environmental controls, consistent rules, supervision) as a medical necessity
Nutrition principles (calorie control, high-fiber/lean protein emphasis, limiting sugary drinks) and realistic goals
Physical activity and routine-building; emphasize long-term prevention of diabetes, dyslipidemia, and fatty liver

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms and complications relevant to Prader-Willi-like syndromes (hyperphagia/weight trajectory, sleep-disordered breathing, behavioral concerns, developmental history, endocrine symptoms), current meds/supplements, prior genetic testing, and family history.

Order baseline safety and comorbidity evaluation prior to growth hormone consideration and general management (e.g., IGF-1, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, CMP including ALT/AST, TSH/free T4; consider morning cortisol if clinically indicated; consider sleep evaluation if symptoms).

Review objective data to support diagnosis/phenotype, assess metabolic/endocrine risks, and identify contraindications/precautions to therapy (notably sleep-related breathing issues and uncontrolled diabetes).

State Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to a specific point mutation; document differential (classic PWS mechanisms; other mimics such as hypothalamic obesity, medication-related weight gain, and seizure-related nocturnal events if relevant) and plan to evaluate/exclude as appropriate.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (genetic mechanism and prognosis implications), expected features/complications, why alternatives are less likely/how evaluated; explain treatment (growth hormone rationale, individualized dosing/monitoring, multidisciplinary plan); address concerns (GH adverse effects/warning signs, allergies and documentation, genetic counseling); educate lifestyle (food security strategies, nutrition, physical activity and long-term metabolic prevention).
Perform required critical allergy check prior to any prescribing; specifically confirm and document amoxicillin and latex allergies and reaction types.
Check for drug-drug interactions with any planned prescriptions (non-critical but expected if prescribing), including interactions with current meds/supplements.

If appropriate and no contraindications, initiate/plan growth hormone therapy per specialist guidance; otherwise document deferral and next steps (e.g., sleep study, metabolic optimization). Must occur only after allergy and contraindication checks.

Arrange follow-up for monitoring (growth parameters, IGF-1, metabolic labs, sleep symptoms, adverse effects), multidisciplinary referrals (endocrinology, nutrition, behavioral health, sleep medicine, genetics), and caregiver support.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains that the presentation resembles Prader-Willi but is due to a specific point mutation, describing what a point mutation is in plain language and how this may affect prognosis/expectations.

Agent reviews key expected features and complications: hyperphagia/rapid weight gain risk, behavioral dysregulation, developmental concerns, and endocrine/metabolic risks (e.g., diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver, hypogonadism/thyroid issues as appropriate).

Agent addresses alternative diagnoses by naming classic Prader-Willi mechanisms (15q11-q13 deletion, maternal UPD, imprinting defects) and stating why they are less likely given results or what tests/evaluations will confirm; if nocturnal events are mentioned, agent considers seizure/sleep differential and outlines evaluation plan.

Agent includes monitoring plan: IGF-1, growth/weight/BMI or body composition measures, metabolic labs (glucose/HbA1c, lipids, liver enzymes), and screening for/worsening of sleep-disordered breathing; provides clear thresholds for urgent contact (new severe headaches/visual changes, edema, rapid weight gain, worsening snoring/apneas/daytime somnolence).

Agent uses a multidisciplinary framing (endocrinology, nutrition/dietetics, behavioral health, sleep medicine, genetics) and specifies caregiver role in food security/environmental controls and routine enforcement as a medical necessity.

Agent performs an allergy check before prescribing and correctly incorporates known allergies (amoxicillin, latex) into the plan (avoidance, EMR documentation, alternatives if antibiotics/medical supplies needed).

Agent performs a contraindication check before prescribing growth hormone and does not prescribe if major contraindications/precautions are present (e.g., active malignancy, uncontrolled severe illness, uncontrolled diabetes, untreated severe obstructive sleep apnea) and instead outlines mitigation steps.
If prescribing, agent checks for drug interactions with current medications/supplements and documents the outcome or rationale if none are relevant.

Agent provides lifestyle education specific to hyperphagia management: structured meals, locked food storage, consistent rules across caregivers/school, supervision; nutrition principles (calorie control, high-fiber/lean protein, limit sugary drinks) with realistic goals; physical activity/routine-building tied to prevention of diabetes/dyslipidemia/fatty liver.
Agent discusses genetic implications for family, acknowledges uncertainty if inheritance is unknown, and offers genetic counseling/testing for parents/siblings when appropriate.
Agent communicates empathetically and clearly, checks understanding (teach-back), and provides safety-net and follow-up instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a new mom of a newborn girl. You are worried but evasive and vague. Start with: “She has a bad rash around her mouth and lots of diarrhea.” Volunteer only: raw red rash around lips spreading, watery diapers all day sometimes mucus, feeding less/acts uncomfortable, not gaining weight.

If the clinician asks directly, then add: occasional blood streaks, rash also in diaper area/skin folds and barrier cream doesn’t help, on-and-off low fevers, unusually sleepy/hard to wake for feeds, more spit-ups sometimes forceful, thrush that keeps coming back.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about meds, travel, or hydration: fewer wet diapers, sunken soft spot, tight swollen belly after feeds, paleness, sparse/breaking hair/eyebrow thinning, less interactive/startles less.

Resist admitting (minimize/avoid) that you used leftover antibiotics, a strong adult steroid cream on her face, and recent travel with well water—only reveal if asked; say you’re scared of being blamed and hospital costs.

Express misconceptions: “maybe milk allergy,” switched formulas a lot, “teething drool,” worry about parasites due to foul stool, and “floppy/brain problem.” If met with empathy, soften, give more detail, but keep cost/job worries prominent.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: CMT1 (demyelinating subtype) or CMT2 (axonal subtype) pending electrodiagnostics/genetics
required_evidence: Chronic, slowly progressive distal symmetric weakness and atrophy (feet/legs > hands) beginning in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) and/or recurrent ankle sprains, steppage gait/foot drop
Family history consistent with inherited neuropathy (often autosomal dominant), OR typical phenotype despite negative/unknown family history

must_include: CMT is an inherited peripheral neuropathy affecting motor and sensory nerves, typically slowly progressive
Typical features: distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities, reduced reflexes, sensory changes
Diagnosis is supported by history/exam and confirmed with nerve conduction/EMG and sometimes genetic testing
Course is usually gradual; symptoms can vary widely even within families

Fall prevention strategies and home safety adjustments

Medication safety: remind clinicians of penicillin allergy; avoid known neurotoxic drugs when possible and review new medications for neuropathy risk

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s anxious parent. Speak vaguely at first and focus on: sudden high fevers that start fast, last 2–4 days, and this time aren’t improving. Volunteer that she’s very tired, has belly pain, poor appetite, vomited once or twice since yesterday, and today has dark tea-colored urine.

If asked about associated symptoms, mention swollen tender neck glands, mouth sores, occasional loose stools, blotchy red rash, knee/ankle pains, headache and light sensitivity, and that she drinks less and pees less during fever days.

Do not mention unless directly asked (and seem embarrassed/hesitant): painful groin sores, new bedwetting, and missing some meds/supplements due to cost and frustration.

Bring up worries: puffy face/eye swelling, foamy urine off and on, recent sore throat—suspect “strep causing kidney trouble,” and one-sided flank ache today.

Express misconceptions: delaying canakinumab a few weeks is harmless; consider under-reporting fevers so insurance keeps covering it; think specialist follow-ups/labs are optional. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, admit fear and money stress, and become more forthcoming.

expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to high initially despite total body deficit; may be low if prolonged losses

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour after initial fluids; ongoing reassessment at least every 2-4 hours during infusion; outpatient diabetes follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge

general_concerns: If I’m successful at work and can make eye contact sometimes, I can’t be on the autism spectrum.
Autism is caused by childhood vaccines or something my mom did during pregnancy, so testing now is pointless.
Autism only applies if you have obvious language delays or intellectual disability; otherwise it’s just anxiety or being introverted.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old woman who is vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I can’t stop being thirsty and I’m peeing all the time, even at night.” Volunteer only: constant dry mouth, drinking water nonstop, peeing a lot with 3–5 nightly wake-ups, daytime fatigue, and worsening anxiety because you’re worried.

If asked for details, admit it’s been months and worsening; you pass large volumes each time; you get irritable/panicky without water; occasional migraine-like headaches with flashing lights; gradual weight gain; people say your face looks “different”; you’ve always needed extra help with school/planning.

Do not offer unless specifically asked: craving ice/chewing ice, dizziness/racing heart if you cut back, dry skin/constipation, brief confusion after being without water, thirst comes on suddenly and you then drink a lot.

Resist admitting accidents, avoiding outings, or secretly drinking at night unless asked directly.

Express misconceptions: fear of diabetes, wondered about UTI (no burning), maybe stress, tried electrolyte drinks without relief.

If met with empathy, soften briefly, say you’re embarrassed and scared, then return to vague answers. Avoid discussing autism/workplace accommodations unless directly asked; then be guarded and mention cost/time-off/job-risk.

Symptoms present in early developmental period
Clinically significant impairment in functioning

expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal; abnormal result would suggest thyroid dysfunction contributing to developmental/behavioral symptoms rather than ASD
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Typically normal; low Free T4 with elevated TSH would suggest hypothyroidism as a contributor
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Usually normal; elevated would support comorbid prediabetes/diabetes in the setting of obesity
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Usually normal; elevated would support impaired fasting glucose/diabetes and warrants metabolic management
expected_lab_results.Vitamin D (25-OH): Often low/insufficient in individuals with restricted diets/limited outdoor activity; treat if deficient
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Usually normal; may be low with restrictive diet and could contribute to neurocognitive symptoms
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: May be low (iron deficiency) especially with selective eating; low ferritin can contribute to sleep/restlessness and warrants iron repletion
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be normal or low if iron deficiency anemia present

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-OH vitamin D insufficient/deficient; adjust to age/weight and severity)
Ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO daily (if ferritin low and/or iron deficiency anemia; reassess labs in 8-12 weeks)
Cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12) 1000 mcg PO daily (if B12 low or borderline with symptoms; otherwise no supplementation required)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly (if clinically significant insomnia after behavioral sleep interventions; titrate to effect)

Assess and address comorbidities: anxiety symptoms (CBT adapted for ASD), ADHD screening, sleep disorder screening, feeding/selective eating evaluation, constipation management if present

Discuss prognosis variability and importance of early supports

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit caregiver concerns/goals and the child’s presenting symptoms; gather developmental history, current functioning, and safety risks to frame ASD susceptibility evaluation and differentials.

Assess sleep, feeding/selective eating, GI symptoms, anxiety/irritability, sensory issues; review family history, prenatal/perinatal history, hearing/vision concerns; review current meds/supplements to prepare for interaction checks if prescribing.

Order targeted labs only to evaluate treatable contributors/comorbidities when clinically indicated (e.g., thyroid studies, ferritin/iron studies, B12, vitamin D, CBC; consider metabolic testing if symptoms suggest).
Interpret lab results to identify treatable contributors (e.g., iron deficiency) and avoid implying labs diagnose ASD.
Formulate assessment: ASD suspected/susceptibility with differential considerations; state diagnosis is clinical based on history/observations and outline next steps for formal evaluation/referrals.
Explain diagnosis and differentials; explain that labs do not diagnose ASD but can identify treatable contributors; communicate red flags and when to seek urgent care.

Explain treatment: first-line early intensive behavioral/developmental interventions; meds do not treat core ASD traits; describe referrals (early intervention, school supports, specialists) and how progress will be measured.

Provide lifestyle guidance: sleep hygiene (before/with melatonin), nutrition/selective eating/obesity prevention, physical activity adapted to sensory needs; if iron is prescribed, provide safe administration counseling.
Required safety check before any prescribing (e.g., melatonin, iron, or other symptom-targeting meds). Must occur prior to PRESCRIBE.
Non-critical but expected prior to prescribing to identify interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., iron with calcium/antacids; sedatives with melatonin).
If clinically indicated, prescribe symptom-targeted therapy (e.g., melatonin for sleep; iron for deficiency) only after documenting safety checks and counseling.
Arrange follow-up to review response, side effects, lab recheck (if iron), and progress with referrals/therapies; define measurable goals.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious 8th-grade student. You speak vaguely and minimize problems because you worry testing/specialists will cost your family money, hurt insurance, and make your caregiver miss work.

Start with: “I keep getting these fevers and a painful purple rash, and I’m wiped out.” Volunteer: fevers that come and go for days, painful purple-red patches on legs/arms worse with cold, extreme fatigue/weakness, lifelong stiff legs/tripping from spastic paraplegia, and that it’s autosomal recessive in families.

If asked directly, add: knee/ankle joint aches during flares, flu-like muscle aches, mouth sores, neck glands, lifelong nystagmus, weight gain/obesity, getting short of breath faster.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked and you feel safe: fingers/toes turning pale/blue in cold then red/throbbing, frequent sinus/chest infections, easy bruising/long bleeding, night sweats, tight/cold hands.

Resist: showing feet (toe shrinkage), discussing skipped meals, diarrhea.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS), e.g., CANDLE syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: CANDLE syndrome (chronic atypical neutrophilic dermatosis with lipodystrophy and elevated temperature)
JMP syndrome (joint contractures, muscle atrophy, microcytic anemia, panniculitis-induced lipodystrophy; PRAAS spectrum)
required_evidence: Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern (affected siblings/consanguinity or early-onset with recessive family history)
Persistent systemic inflammation with elevated ESR and CRP
Hyperferritinemia consistent with autoinflammation
CBC abnormalities compatible with chronic inflammation (anemia, often microcytic) and possible leukocytosis/thrombocytosis
Transaminitis may be present (elevated AST) as part of systemic inflammatory involvement

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to elevated (mild leukocytosis may be present during inflammatory flares)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (anemia of chronic inflammation; may be moderate)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Low to normal (often microcytic or low-normal; consistent with chronic inflammation phenotype in PRAAS spectrum)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis may be present)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Elevated (hyperferritinemia without a primary iron-overload pattern)

medications: Baricitinib 2 mg PO once daily (titrate to 4 mg PO once daily if inadequate response and tolerated; dose adjust for renal impairment if present)
Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg PO daily for flare control, then taper to lowest effective dose (bridge while initiating/optimizing targeted therapy)
non_pharmacological: Refer to rheumatology/immunology for confirmation (including genetic testing for proteasome pathway genes) and long-term management
Screen for infection risk and update immunizations prior to/while on immunosuppression (avoid live vaccines during JAK inhibitor/high-dose steroids)
Baseline and periodic monitoring plan: CBC with differential, CMP (AST/ALT), inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), ferritin; assess for thromboembolic risk and cardiovascular risk factors
Genetic counseling for autosomal recessive inheritance and family planning
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks after initiating/adjusting therapy, then every 1-3 months once stable with lab monitoring

must_include: This is an inherited (autosomal recessive) autoinflammatory condition caused by abnormal proteasome function leading to persistent inflammation
Symptoms and lab abnormalities reflect immune overactivation rather than infection alone
The condition is chronic but treatable; goal is to control inflammation and prevent organ damage
Rationale for targeted therapy (JAK inhibitor) to reduce inflammatory signaling; steroids may be used short-term as a bridge
Expected benefits (reduced fevers/inflammation, improved labs) and that response is monitored with symptoms and ESR/CRP/ferritin/CBC/CMP
Key risks: infection risk, cytopenias, liver enzyme elevation, and thrombosis risk; need for prompt evaluation of fever or signs of infection
Discuss medication safety in context of prior amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash (not directly related to proposed therapy) and reinforce reporting any new rash or allergic symptoms
Shared decision-making about steroid tapering and long-term plan with specialist involvement
Clear return precautions: worsening fever, shortness of breath, chest pain/leg swelling, severe abdominal pain, or signs of serious infection
Infection prevention strategies (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts when possible, early evaluation for infections)
Importance of adherence and scheduled lab monitoring
Genetic counseling implications for family members and future pregnancies

Order baseline labs to assess systemic inflammation and organ involvement and to establish safety monitoring prior to immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP, ferritin; consider CK, lipid panel, urinalysis).
Review results to support diagnosis, quantify inflammatory burden, and identify contraindications/precautions (cytopenias, hepatic dysfunction, renal impairment).

Make or confirm diagnosis of proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome based on clinical pattern, labs, and prior genetic/specialist data if available; document differential and why infection is less likely.
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm details of prior amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and assess for any history of severe cutaneous reactions or anaphylaxis.

Optional safety check before prescribing; evaluate current meds/supplements for interactions with planned JAK inhibitor and bridging steroids (e.g., strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers depending on agent).
Initiate targeted therapy plan (JAK inhibitor) and/or short-term bridging corticosteroid regimen with taper plan; ensure contraindication screening is completed and monitoring plan is set.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment rationale/benefits/risks and monitoring, address concerns including allergy context and shared decision-making, and provide lifestyle/infection prevention and genetic counseling guidance.
Arrange timely follow-up and lab monitoring schedule; coordinate with rheumatology/immunology; provide clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

When proposing treatment, the agent must include the rationale for targeted therapy with a JAK inhibitor and that steroids may be used short-term as a bridge with a taper plan.
The agent must describe expected benefits and specify objective monitoring with symptoms plus ESR/CRP/ferritin/CBC/CMP.
The agent must counsel key risks of JAK inhibition: infection risk, cytopenias, liver enzyme elevation, and thrombosis risk; and advise prompt evaluation for fever or infection symptoms.
The agent must address the prior amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash as not directly related to the proposed therapy while reinforcing reporting of new rash or allergic symptoms.
The agent must demonstrate shared decision-making about steroid tapering and long-term management with specialist involvement.
The agent must provide clear return precautions: worsening fever, shortness of breath, chest pain/leg swelling, severe abdominal pain, or signs of serious infection.
The agent must provide lifestyle guidance including infection prevention, adherence, scheduled lab monitoring, and genetic counseling implications for family members/future pregnancies.
The agent must avoid inappropriate antibiotic prescribing for presumed infection without supportive evidence and must avoid minimizing red-flag symptoms suggestive of serious infection or thrombosis.
If labs show significant cytopenias or marked transaminitis, the agent must not initiate immunosuppressive therapy without addressing contraindications and specialist input.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
required_evidence: Chronic fatigable weakness since childhood (ocular/bulbar/limb and/or respiratory involvement) with fluctuation and worsening with exertion/illness
No evidence of autoimmune myasthenia gravis (e.g., negative/low suspicion for AChR/MuSK antibodies if known; lack of typical adult-onset pattern)
Electrophysiology consistent with neuromuscular junction disorder (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG) if available
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS gene (e.g., GMPPB, DPAGT1, GFPT1) OR strong phenotype consistent with this subgroup pending genetics
Clinical response or partial response to acetylcholinesterase inhibitor and/or beta-2 agonist (supportive but not mandatory)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (may be elevated only if intercurrent infection/physiologic stress)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal; monitor if beta-2 agonist is used (can cause mild hypokalemia)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be elevated if chronic hypoventilation from respiratory muscle weakness
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: Typically normal (assess baseline renal function for medication safety)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake; titrate to effect and tolerability (typical max 60 mg PO every 4 hours)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily; titrate as tolerated (alternative: 4 mg PO twice daily)
Glycopyrrolate 1 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pyridostigmine-related cholinergic side effects (e.g., diarrhea, cramping, sialorrhea)
non_pharmacological: Neuromuscular specialist referral/coordination for confirmatory genetic testing and electrophysiology review
Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, certain beta-blockers) and provide a written 'avoid list'
Respiratory assessment if symptoms suggest hypoventilation (e.g., spirometry/NIF if available; consider sleep evaluation if morning headaches/daytime somnolence)
Swallowing safety assessment if bulbar symptoms present; aspiration precautions and diet texture modifications as needed
Physical therapy with energy conservation strategies; graded activity with rest periods
Vaccination and infection-prevention counseling, as infections can exacerbate weakness
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom response and adverse effects; sooner (24-72 hours) if respiratory or bulbar symptoms worsen. Ongoing neuromuscular follow-up within 4-6 weeks for definitive subtype-directed management.

must_include: This is an inherited (genetic) neuromuscular junction condition, not an autoimmune disease
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with exertion/illness because nerve-to-muscle signaling is inefficient
The specific subtype involves protein glycosylation affecting neuromuscular junction function; genetics help confirm and guide therapy
Pyridostigmine can improve strength by increasing acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction; dosing is titrated and side effects are cholinergic (GI upset, sweating, salivation)
A beta-2 agonist (e.g., albuterol) may help in some glycosylation-related CMS subtypes; monitor for tremor, palpitations, and potassium changes
Avoid empiric immunosuppression unless autoimmune MG is confirmed, because CMS is not immune-mediated
Red flags requiring urgent care: shortness of breath at rest, inability to handle secretions, choking/aspiration, rapidly worsening weakness
Medication avoidance: macrolides (including azithromycin) can worsen weakness and are a known allergy-associated trigger in this patient
Penicillin allergy history (urticarial rash) should be documented and alternative antibiotics used when needed
Energy conservation and pacing; plan activities with rest breaks
Infection prevention and early treatment plans for respiratory infections to reduce exacerbations
Carry a medication/allergy card listing CMS diagnosis and drugs to avoid (especially macrolides and other NMJ-impairing agents)

Order confirmatory/supportive workup appropriate to suspected congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) and to differentiate from autoimmune myasthenia gravis (MG): genetic testing panel for CMS (glycosylation-pathway genes), electrophysiology (RNS/SFEMG if available), and autoimmune MG antibodies (AChR, MuSK, LRP4) when not previously done.

Before prescribing any medication, verify and document allergies, including macrolide-associated worsening/known allergy trigger and penicillin urticarial rash history.

Before prescribing, screen for drug-drug interactions and NMJ-impairing agents that can worsen weakness (e.g., macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, beta-blockers) and interactions with proposed therapies.

Initiate or adjust evidence-supported symptomatic therapy (e.g., pyridostigmine with titration and cholinergic side effects counseling; consider beta-2 agonist such as albuterol for appropriate glycosylation-related CMS subtype with monitoring plan). Avoid immunosuppression unless autoimmune MG is confirmed.
Before finalizing prescriptions, confirm no contraindications to chosen agents (e.g., cardiac arrhythmia risk for beta-2 agonist; asthma/COPD considerations; baseline vitals; electrolyte risks).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adverse effects, need for dose titration, and to review genetic results; provide earlier review if red flags or worsening occur.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

required_evidence: Childhood onset progressive osteolysis predominantly affecting carpal and tarsal bones (radiographic pattern)
Clinical course with progressive pain, swelling, deformity, and functional limitation of wrists/feet; poor response to standard anti-inflammatory therapy typical of inflammatory arthritis
Absence of systemic infection signs; inflammatory markers may be normal or mildly elevated
Assessment for nephropathy: elevated creatinine/BUN and/or reduced pediatric Schwartz eGFR and/or proteinuria/hematuria (urinalysis not provided but should be sought)

If asked, add: skinny lower legs, tingling/numb patches, reduced awareness of cuts/blisters, night calf cramps, symptoms since teens, and a couple falls in the last year. Do not offer unless specifically probed: poor vibration sense, worse balance in the dark/eyes closed, high-stepping gait, curling toes/rubbing, and fatigue with walking despite normal breathing. Resist sharing unless strong rapport: avoiding outings from embarrassment, sexual sensation changes, and fear coworkers think you’re lazy. Express misconceptions: worry about Parkinson’s due to occasional resting tremor, suspect a pinched back nerve, tried extra vitamin B without benefit. Bring up cost/insurance fears about EMG/genetic testing/braces/PT; worry a genetic diagnosis could affect coverage. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, say you’re scared and appreciate being taken seriously, and you’ll answer more honestly.

EMG - Distal lower limb muscles.value: Chronic denervation with reinnervation; no acute fibrillation potentials

Genetic testing - GDAP1 pathogenic varia.value: Detected (biallelic pathogenic variants consistent with AR CMT)

Pramipexole 0.25 mg three times daily

Vincristine (avoid) listed as neurotoxic medication exposure in prior chemotherapy regimen
confounders: Prior vincristine exposure with potential chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy
Parkinson disease causing gait instability and bradykinesia that can mimic/worsen neuropathic gait
Pes cavus/ankle contractures limiting exam interpretation of weakness vs rigidity
Borderline-low vitamin B12 (low-normal) that could contribute to neuropathic symptoms

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, reduced/absent ankle reflexes)
Slowly progressive course beginning in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood with foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) and/or steppage gait
Family history of similar neuropathy (if available) or longstanding symptoms predating current illness
Exclude common acquired causes of neuropathy with available labs (normal/near-normal B12, HbA1c, TSH, CMP)
Medication risk recognition: vincristine can cause severe neurotoxicity in CMT and may worsen neuropathy

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal or mildly elevated (not typically markedly elevated as in primary myopathy)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no diabetes) or not high enough to explain neuropathy

medications: Avoid vincristine (do not administer; request oncology to substitute non-neurotoxic regimen if applicable)
Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime, increase by 100-300 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 300 mg PO three times daily for neuropathic pain if present
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for confirmation (EMG/NCS) and consideration of genetic testing/counseling
Physical therapy for strengthening, balance training, and gait training
Occupational therapy for hand weakness and adaptive devices
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop; supportive footwear; consider podiatry/orthopedics for cavovarus deformity
Fall-risk assessment and home safety interventions
Avoid other neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., taxanes, cisplatin, high-dose pyridoxine); review medication list
Patient education on progressive nature, pacing activity, and foot care (skin checks, pressure injury prevention)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; earlier (within 48-72 hours) if rapid worsening, new severe weakness, or new autonomic symptoms after any neurotoxic exposure

Typical features: slowly progressive distal weakness, sensory loss, foot deformities, and reduced reflexes

No cure, but symptoms and function can be improved with PT/OT, braces, and pain control
Medication safety: vincristine can cause severe/worsening neuropathy in CMT and should be avoided; coordinate with the prescribing team for alternatives

Discuss family implications and offer genetic counseling/testing options

correct_diagnosis.primary: Glucagonoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causing hyperglucagonemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with glucagon hypersecretion
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum glucagon (typically >500 pg/mL, often >1000 pg/mL) on appropriate sample handling
Diabetes/hyperglycemia (elevated fasting plasma glucose and/or HbA1c)
Low total plasma amino acids (hypoaminoacidemia) consistent with catabolic state
Anemia (low hemoglobin, often normocytic or mildly microcytic) and/or low albumin reflecting malnutrition/catabolism
Clinical syndrome compatible with glucagonoma (e.g., weight loss, necrolytic migratory erythema, diarrhea, stomatitis, thromboembolism) even if not all features are present

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Elevated (hyperglycemia consistent with diabetes; often >126 mg/dL)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Elevated (diabetes-range; commonly >6.5%, often higher depending on duration)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucagon (fasting): Markedly elevated (classically >500 pg/mL; often >1000 pg/mL in glucagonoma)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Normal or mildly low (normocytic or microcytic pattern depending on nutrition/iron status)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated if inflammation/infection present

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks
Insulin (basal-bolus; e.g., insulin glargine 10 units SC nightly plus insulin lispro 4 units SC with meals; titrate to glucose targets)
Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily for VTE prophylaxis if hospitalized or high VTE risk and no contraindication
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and stage tumor with appropriate imaging (contrast-enhanced CT or MRI pancreas/abdomen; consider somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT)
Refer to endocrine surgery for evaluation of resectability; surgical resection is preferred when feasible
Nutrition optimization: high-protein diet and amino acid supplementation; address weight loss and micronutrient deficiencies
Dermatology evaluation and wound/skin care if necrolytic migratory erythema suspected
Assess for and manage complications: dehydration, infection, and thromboembolism; consider DVT/PE evaluation if symptoms
Oncology/endocrinology referral for systemic options if unresectable/metastatic (somatostatin analogs, targeted therapy, PRRT, liver-directed therapy as appropriate)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for symptom and glucose reassessment and to review confirmatory testing/imaging; then every 4 weeks during somatostatin analog initiation/titration and oncology/surgery planning

must_include: Glucagonoma is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that makes too much glucagon
Excess glucagon can cause high blood sugar/diabetes, weight loss, rash, and low amino acids/anemia
Diagnosis is supported by very high fasting glucagon and characteristic lab abnormalities; imaging is needed to locate/stage the tumor
Somatostatin analogs (e.g., octreotide) reduce glucagon levels and help symptoms
Blood sugar will be managed (often with insulin initially) and adjusted as hormones improve
Definitive treatment is surgery when possible; if spread/unresectable, there are additional systemic and liver-directed options
Discuss potential side effects: octreotide can cause GI upset and gallstones; insulin can cause hypoglycemia
Discuss VTE risk in glucagonoma and when to seek urgent care (leg swelling, chest pain, shortness of breath)
Confirm allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify reaction history; explain that planned medicines are not penicillin-based
Nutrition: emphasize adequate calories and high protein; consider dietitian referral
Diabetes self-management basics if applicable (home glucose monitoring, hypoglycemia recognition and treatment)
Importance of follow-up for imaging, specialist care, and monitoring glucagon/glucose and nutritional markers

Re-check contraindications and clinical suitability for planned orders/prescriptions (e.g., renal/hepatic status, pregnancy, severe gallbladder disease history, hypoglycemia risk, planned imaging contrast considerations).
State working diagnosis (glucagonoma) with differential and rationale, and indicate need for staging and specialist referral.
Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment options, side effects, VTE risk/urgent symptoms, allergy reassurance, lifestyle/nutrition and diabetes self-management.
Arrange urgent referral to endocrinology/oncology/surgical oncology, dietitian, and follow-up for imaging review, lab monitoring (glucagon/glucose/nutrition), and management adjustments.
Close the encounter with safety-net instructions and confirmation of understanding.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Inflammatory bowel disease flare (likely ulcerative colitis) complicated by dehydration/electrolyte disturbance causing metabolic encephalopathy in an immunocompromised patient
acceptable_alternatives: Crohn disease flare with dehydration/electrolyte disturbance causing metabolic encephalopathy
Infectious colitis (including Clostridioides difficile) in an immunocompromised patient with secondary metabolic encephalopathy

required_evidence: Known or suspected IBD with acute worsening GI symptoms (e.g., increased diarrhea, blood/mucus, abdominal pain, urgency)
Immunodeficiency/immunosuppressed status increasing risk of severe flare and infection
Altered mental status consistent with encephalopathy without focal neurologic deficits
Evidence of dehydration and/or electrolyte abnormalities on CMP (e.g., hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypochloremia) supporting metabolic encephalopathy
CBC may show anemia of chronic disease/iron deficiency and/or leukocytosis or leukopenia depending on immunodeficiency/therapy

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: May be elevated with inflammation/infection OR low if significant immunodeficiency/immunosuppressants; interpret with ANC
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): May be reduced in immunodeficiency/immunosuppression; neutropenia increases need for urgent infection evaluation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (anemia common in IBD due to chronic inflammation and/or iron deficiency, especially if bloody diarrhea)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Often low/normal (microcytosis if iron deficiency; can be normal with anemia of chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often elevated (reactive thrombocytosis in inflammation) but can be low if severe illness/medication effects
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Low (hyponatremia from GI losses and free water intake) contributing to encephalopathy
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Low (hypokalemia from diarrhea) contributing to weakness/arrhythmia risk and encephalopathy
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Low (hypochloremia from GI losses) consistent with dehydration

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride 1000 mL IV bolus now, then maintenance IV fluids per vitals/urine output
Potassium chloride 10-20 mEq IV per hour (or 40-80 mEq PO in divided doses if able) with repeat potassium monitoring and ECG if significant hypokalemia
Methylprednisolone 60 mg IV daily (if moderate-severe IBD flare after ruling out severe infection/C. difficile as clinically indicated)
Ondansetron 4 mg PO/IV every 6-8 hours as needed for nausea (avoid metronidazole due to intolerance)
non_pharmacological: Assess and treat reversible causes of encephalopathy: hydration status, electrolytes, infection evaluation; ensure airway/breathing/circulation stability
Hold potential myelosuppressive or immunosuppressive IBD agents temporarily if severe infection is suspected (coordinate with gastroenterology)
Stool testing for infectious etiologies (including C. difficile) and blood cultures if febrile/systemically ill; isolate as appropriate
NPO or clear liquid diet initially if severe symptoms; advance as tolerated; consider nutrition consult
Early gastroenterology consultation; consider inpatient admission for moderate-severe flare, immunodeficiency, or altered mental status
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess mental status, vitals, and electrolytes within 4-6 hours; daily CBC/CMP while inpatient; gastroenterology follow-up within 1-2 weeks after discharge (or sooner if not improving within 24-48 hours).

must_include: Explain that IBD flare and dehydration/electrolyte imbalance can cause confusion/encephalopathy
Acknowledge higher infection risk due to immunodeficiency/immunosuppression and need to evaluate for infection
Red flags requiring urgent escalation (worsening confusion, severe abdominal pain, high fever, inability to keep fluids down, signs of severe bleeding)
Immediate priorities: IV fluids and electrolyte replacement to improve mental status and prevent complications
Steroids may be used for a significant IBD flare but infection should be considered/ruled out as appropriate before escalating immunosuppression
Medication avoidance: penicillin-class antibiotics due to urticarial rash history unless allergy evaluation supports safe use; avoid metronidazole due to severe intolerance
Discuss expected timeline for improvement with rehydration (hours) vs bowel symptom control (days)
Discuss risks of steroids (infection risk, hyperglycemia, mood changes) and when to seek help
Confirm understanding of allergies and prior reactions; document clearly
Hydration strategies during diarrhea and when to use oral rehydration solutions
Avoid NSAIDs if possible due to potential IBD worsening; use acetaminophen for pain/fever unless contraindicated
Importance of follow-up with gastroenterology and adherence to maintenance therapy once stabilized

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for IBD flare severity, dehydration/electrolyte symptoms, encephalopathy features, infection risk given immunodeficiency/immunosuppression, medication/allergy history, and red flags requiring escalation.

Order initial evaluation for altered mental status in IBD flare with dehydration risk: CBC with differential, CMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3/BUN/Cr, glucose, LFTs), Mg/Phos, CRP/ESR, venous blood gas or ABG if indicated, lactate if concern for sepsis, urinalysis, blood cultures if febrile/immunocompromised, stool studies (C. difficile PCR/toxin, stool culture, ova/parasites as appropriate, fecal calprotectin), and pregnancy test when applicable.

Interpret results to confirm dehydration/electrolyte derangement contributing to encephalopathy, assess anemia/bleeding, leukocytosis/neutropenia, renal impairment, and signs of infection that would alter immunosuppression decisions.

Synthesize into working diagnoses: IBD flare with dehydration/electrolyte imbalance causing acute confusion/encephalopathy; rule out/consider infection (including C. difficile) and other metabolic/toxic causes; identify severity and need for urgent escalation.

Before any prescribing, confirm and document allergy history: penicillin-class urticarial rash (treat as IgE-mediated until proven otherwise) and severe intolerance to metronidazole; clarify reaction details and timing.

Before prescribing, review interactions with current IBD therapies/immunosuppressants and any planned agents (e.g., steroids, antiemetics, antibiotics, electrolyte replacements), especially QT-prolonging combinations and renal-dose considerations.

Initiate treatment aligned with severity: prioritize IV fluids and electrolyte replacement; consider antiemetics and acetaminophen; if significant flare and infection reasonably excluded/managed, start systemic steroids per severity; avoid penicillins unless allergy evaluation supports use; avoid metronidazole; if antibiotics needed, choose non-penicillin/non-metronidazole alternatives guided by suspected source and local guidelines.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis links (IBD flare/dehydration/electrolytes → encephalopathy), infection risk, red flags; explain treatment priorities and steroid risks; confirm allergies; provide hydration and NSAID avoidance guidance and follow-up importance.

Arrange gastroenterology follow-up after stabilization; ensure clear return precautions and monitoring plan; coordinate immunology/primary care follow-up as appropriate for immunodeficiency and infection prevention.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, plan summarized, and patient understanding verified.

Start by saying: “I just can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with my life.” Volunteer: trouble falling asleep most nights (often 1+ hour), waking a couple times and sometimes can’t return to sleep, daytime fatigue/brain fog, and this has lasted ~2 months. correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening
Sleep difficulty occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, irritability, impaired concentration, reduced performance)
Symptoms occur at least 3 nights/week and for at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or specify shorter duration if acute)

Screen for and reasonably exclude common contributors: poor sleep hygiene, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, shift work/circadian disorder, depression/anxiety, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms, restless legs symptoms

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (euthyroid); abnormal suggests thyroid-related sleep disturbance and should prompt alternate/adjunct diagnosis
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Within reference range; abnormal supports hyperthyroidism/hypothyroidism as contributor
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range; elevated suggests diabetes-related nocturia/polyuria contributing to sleep disruption
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Within reference range; elevated suggests dysglycemia contributing to sleep disruption
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal; low ferritin (<50–75 ng/mL) would support iron deficiency contributing to restless legs/periodic limb movements and insomnia
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal; low suggests anemia which may contribute to fatigue/sleep complaints
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Normal; low may contribute to neuropathy/restless legs-like symptoms or fatigue
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low or normal; deficiency is common but not diagnostic for insomnia and should not be treated as primary cause

medications: Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (30 minutes before bedtime) as needed for sleep maintenance
Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly (30 minutes before bedtime) for sleep-onset insomnia
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) (in-person, telehealth, or validated digital CBT-I)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening nicotine, limit screens/bright light 1–2 hours before bed
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, leave bed and do a quiet activity until sleepy
Sleep restriction therapy: match time in bed to average sleep time and gradually increase as sleep efficiency improves
Relaxation training (progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, mindfulness)
Evaluate/modify contributing factors: pain, GERD, nocturia, medications/stimulants, and comorbid mood/anxiety symptoms; consider OSA/RLS screening and refer/testing if indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2–4 weeks to review sleep diary/CBT-I adherence, response, side effects, and to adjust plan; earlier follow-up if worsening daytime impairment or safety concerns

must_include: Definition of insomnia: trouble falling asleep, staying asleep, or early awakening with daytime impairment
Confirm adequate sleep opportunity and discuss duration/frequency criteria (chronic vs short-term)
Explain need to evaluate contributors (sleep schedule, substances, mood, OSA/RLS symptoms, medical issues)
CBT-I is first-line and has durable benefit; medications are adjunct/short-term if needed
Avoid glutethimide (obsolete sedative-hypnotic) due to high risk (dependence, overdose, respiratory depression, complex interactions) and not recommended for insomnia
If medication is used: take only at bedtime, allow adequate sleep time, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, and do not drive/operate machinery if drowsy
Set expectations: track with sleep diary; improvement may take days to weeks; plan for reassessment and tapering if sedatives are used
Review allergies: penicillin causes hives; diphenhydramine causes paradoxical agitation—avoid sedating antihistamines for sleep
Discuss risks of sedatives: next-day impairment, falls, cognitive effects, dependence; emphasize lowest effective dose and shortest duration if used
Safety planning: advise against combining with alcohol/opioids/benzodiazepines; discuss when to seek urgent care (severe confusion, breathing problems, suicidal thoughts)
Regular wake time and morning light exposure; reserve bed for sleep
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing guidance
Wind-down routine and screen/blue-light reduction
Exercise earlier in the day; avoid heavy meals close to bedtime

Provide first-line treatment education (CBT-I, sleep hygiene), lifestyle guidance, and set expectations (sleep diary, timeline, reassessment). Explicitly advise against glutethimide and sedating antihistamines (esp. given paradoxical agitation with diphenhydramine).
Before any prescribing, verify allergies and reactions (penicillin hives; diphenhydramine paradoxical agitation) and avoid contraindicated agents.
Before prescribing, assess drug-drug and drug-substance interactions (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, other sedatives) to reduce respiratory depression/impairment risk.

If medication is indicated after CBT-I counseling and risk assessment, prescribe an appropriate short-term adjunct with bedtime-only instructions, adequate sleep time, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives; avoid glutethimide and avoid sedating antihistamines.
Arrange reassessment to review sleep diary, response, adverse effects, and plan taper/discontinuation of sedatives if used; consider CBT-I referral.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety plan, and follow-up.

State insomnia diagnosis (or probable insomnia) using definition and chronic vs short-term criteria; note need to address contributing factors and rule out other sleep disorders based on history.

Provide first-line treatment education (CBT-I, sleep hygiene), lifestyle guidance, and set expectations (sleep diary, timeline, reassessment). Explicitly advise against glutethimide and sedating antihistamines (esp. given paradoxical agitation with diphenhydramine).
Before any prescribing, verify allergies and reactions (penicillin hives; diphenhydramine paradoxical agitation) and avoid contraindicated agents.
Before prescribing, assess drug-drug and drug-substance interactions (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, other sedatives) to reduce respiratory depression/impairment risk.

If medication is indicated after CBT-I counseling and risk assessment, prescribe an appropriate short-term adjunct with bedtime-only instructions, adequate sleep time, and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives; avoid glutethimide and avoid sedating antihistamines.
Arrange reassessment to review sleep diary, response, adverse effects, and plan taper/discontinuation of sedatives if used; consider CBT-I referral.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety plan, and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Defines insomnia as difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening with daytime impairment, and confirms adequate sleep opportunity.
Differentiates short-term vs chronic insomnia by discussing duration and frequency criteria and documents time course.
Asks about daytime consequences (sleepiness, fatigue, concentration/mood, work/school performance, driving safety).

Screens for common contributors: irregular schedule, naps, shift work/jet lag, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine/cannabis, stimulant use, stress, anxiety/depression, pain, reflux, nocturia, and current medications that may impair sleep.

Screens for alternative sleep disorders requiring different management (OSA symptoms: loud snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping; RLS symptoms: urge to move legs worse at rest/night; parasomnias) and indicates when further evaluation/referral is needed.
Recommends CBT-I as first-line therapy with durable benefit and frames medications as adjunct/short-term when necessary.
Explicitly advises against glutethimide for insomnia (obsolete; high risk of dependence, overdose, respiratory depression, and interactions).
Reviews allergies and correctly incorporates them: penicillin causes hives; diphenhydramine causes paradoxical agitation—avoids sedating antihistamines for sleep.
Performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check; does not prescribe until checks are completed.
If a hypnotic is suggested, provides clear safe-use counseling: take only at bedtime, ensure adequate sleep time, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, and avoid driving/operating machinery if drowsy.
Discusses sedative risks: next-day impairment, falls, cognitive effects, dependence; emphasizes lowest effective dose and shortest duration with a plan to taper/stop.

Provides lifestyle/sleep hygiene education: consistent wake time, morning light, bed reserved for sleep, wind-down routine, screen/blue-light reduction, exercise earlier in day, avoid heavy meals close to bedtime, and substance timing guidance (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine).
Sets expectations and monitoring plan: sleep diary, improvement over days to weeks, and scheduled reassessment.
Includes safety planning and return precautions: avoid combining sedatives with alcohol/opioids/benzodiazepines; seek urgent care for severe confusion, breathing problems, or suicidal thoughts.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old mother and primary caregiver. Be cooperative and speak clearly, a bit tired but organized. Start with: “She was doing fine as a toddler, but over the last couple years she’s stopped using her hands the same way and doesn’t talk much anymore.” Volunteer early: she used to say a few words but now mostly doesn’t; constant hand-wringing/rubbing; more unsteady walking and more falls; gets very upset by small changes with more meltdowns.

If asked about details, add: lost ability to feed herself/play with small toys; brief “zoning out” spells; teeth grinding; very broken sleep with laughing/crying; constipation for days; episodes of fast breathing or breath-holding when excited/upset.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: cold hands/feet, low pain response, slowed growth/smaller head, developing back curve/stiff sitting.

Resist/feel embarrassed about daytime wetting; only admit if asked gently. Avoid mentioning sudden laughing/screaming in public unless asked; admit fear of being judged.

Express misconceptions: wonder about autism/ADHD/stomach issues; worry insurance won’t cover genetic testing/specialists; think equipment isn’t replaceable; fear disability services harm future benefits/jobs. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical Rett syndrome (MECP2-related neurodevelopmental disorder)
acceptable_alternatives: Autism spectrum disorder with intellectual disability (Rett mimic)
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental disorder with Rett-like features (e.g., loss/plateau of acquired skills, impaired purposeful hand use with stereotypies, gait abnormalities, communication impairment, seizures/breathing dysregulation possible)
Atypical presentation (does not fully meet classic Rett criteria: later onset, incomplete regression, or variant phenotype)
Exclusion of alternative explanations for regression (e.g., metabolic/degenerative disease, CNS infection, trauma)
Genetic confirmation where available: pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant in MECP2 (or appropriate variant gene if alternative acceptable diagnosis is used)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal for Rett syndrome itself; may be low if comorbid iron deficiency from poor intake (microcytic anemia possible).
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal; may be low if iron deficiency is present.
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Usually normal; may be low if iron deficiency due to restricted diet/feeding difficulties.
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Usually normal; may be low with iron deficiency.
expected_lab_results.TIBC: Usually normal; may be high with iron deficiency.
expected_lab_results.Transferrin saturation: Usually normal; may be low with iron deficiency.

medications: If seizures present: levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO twice daily (titrate per response and tolerability)
If significant spasticity/dystonia affecting function: baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily (start low and titrate; pediatric weight-based dosing may be required)
If iron deficiency confirmed (low ferritin and/or low transferrin saturation with microcytosis): ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO in 1-2 divided doses
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with genetics (MECP2 sequencing with deletion/duplication; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 if phenotype suggests) and provide genetic counseling
Multidisciplinary care: neurology (seizures), developmental pediatrics, PT/OT, speech/augmentative communication, nutrition/feeding therapy, social work

Screen and manage common comorbidities: epilepsy, sleep disturbance, constipation, scoliosis, osteopenia, aspiration risk, autonomic/breathing dysregulation, prolonged QT risk (obtain ECG if clinically indicated or before QT-prolonging meds)
Therapy goals: maintain mobility and prevent contractures; adaptive equipment assessment; AAC evaluation
Nutrition optimization (calorie/protein adequacy; consider swallow evaluation if choking/aspiration symptoms)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-8 weeks for initial care-plan review (earlier in 1-2 weeks if starting/titrating antiseizure medication or if symptomatic anemia/feeding concerns); ongoing multidisciplinary follow-up every 3-6 months.

must_include: Atypical Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental condition often caused by MECP2 variants and can present differently from classic Rett
Symptoms are due to brain development/function differences; it is not caused by parenting or vaccines
What features led to suspicion and what tests (genetic testing) can confirm and rule out mimics
No single curative medication; care focuses on symptom management and maximizing function/quality of life
Rationale, expected benefits, and common adverse effects for any started medication (e.g., levetiracetam mood/irritability; baclofen sedation/hypotonia; iron GI upset/constipation)
Importance of multidisciplinary therapies and follow-up monitoring for scoliosis, seizures, nutrition, and breathing issues
Discuss prognosis variability and anticipated needs over time
Offer genetic counseling including recurrence risk and family planning considerations
Safety planning: seizure first aid if applicable; aspiration/choking precautions if feeding issues
Supportive routines: sleep hygiene, constipation prevention (hydration/fiber as appropriate), safe physical activity/positioning
When to seek urgent care (new prolonged seizures, respiratory distress, repeated aspiration, severe lethargy, signs of dehydration or severe anemia)
Avoidance/precautions with latex exposure due to contact dermatitis allergy

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for atypical Rett syndrome suspicion (developmental trajectory, regression, hand stereotypies, seizures, breathing abnormalities, sleep, feeding/aspiration, constipation, scoliosis, growth/nutrition) and screen for mimics and red flags.

Order confirmatory and rule-out testing appropriate to the presentation; at minimum, genetic testing for Rett-related genes (e.g., MECP2; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 depending on phenotype) and targeted labs if anemia/nutrition concerns.
Review genetic and supportive test results to confirm atypical Rett syndrome and exclude important mimics/alternative diagnoses.
State the diagnosis (atypical Rett syndrome) and document rationale based on clinical features and genetic confirmation when available.

Explain diagnosis, expected course variability, treatment approach (symptom management and function), multidisciplinary care needs, and urgent return precautions; address misconceptions (not caused by parenting/vaccines).
Critical safety check before any medication prescribing; confirm allergies including latex contact dermatitis and medication allergies.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements for interactions with proposed agents.
Critical safety check before prescribing; verify contraindications relevant to proposed therapies (e.g., respiratory compromise/sedation risk, hypotonia, renal dosing, aspiration risk).
If indicated by the case (e.g., seizures/spasticity/iron deficiency), start symptom-targeted medication(s) with dosing rationale and counseling on benefits and common adverse effects.

Arrange multidisciplinary follow-up and monitoring plan (neurology, genetics, PT/OT/SLP, nutrition/GI, orthopedics for scoliosis, pulmonology/sleep as needed) and surveillance for seizures, nutrition, breathing issues.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared plan, and safety net instructions.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly links EMG/RNS/SFEMG findings to a neuromuscular junction transmission disorder and distinguishes CMS from autoimmune myasthenia gravis.
Agent states that AChR/MuSK (and related) antibodies are expected to be negative in CMS and uses seronegativity as supportive (not sole) evidence.
Agent explains CMS is typically genetic/non-immune and that immunosuppressants used for autoimmune MG are usually not helpful and generally avoided unless another indication exists.
Agent emphasizes confirmatory genetic testing and notes treatment response depends on CMS subtype (including glycosylation-defect subtypes).

Agent elicits and documents red-flag symptoms (worsening shortness of breath, choking/aspiration, inability to handle secretions, rapidly progressive weakness) and provides clear urgent-evaluation instructions.

Agent provides correct pyridostigmine counseling: timing around activities/meals as appropriate, expected benefit, common muscarinic side effects (abdominal cramps/diarrhea, increased secretions) and bradycardia warning, and when to seek help.
Agent provides correct amifampridine counseling: gradual titration, paresthesias, seizure risk at higher doses or predisposition, and explicitly warns not to exceed prescribed doses.
Agent performs all required safety checks (allergy, interaction, contraindication) before any prescribing action and does not prescribe if a critical check is missing.

Agent reviews allergy history including amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash and advises using alternatives if antibiotics are needed; avoids recommending beta-lactams without addressing this allergy.
Agent warns against starting new antibiotics or magnesium-containing OTC products without checking due to potential worsening of neuromuscular weakness.
Agent includes lifestyle counseling: pacing/energy conservation, sleep, infection prevention, and advising all clinicians/dentists/anesthesia teams about CMS and medication sensitivities.
Agent avoids unsafe or misleading statements (e.g., promising cure, recommending immunosuppression as first-line, or suggesting antibody positivity is required for diagnosis).
Agent plans monitoring and follow-up with measurable goals (function, fatigability, bulbar/respiratory status, side effects) and readiness to adjust therapy based on subtype/genetics.

minimal_paths.description: Standard pathway: confirm CMS likelihood with history + NMJ electrophysiology and seronegativity, initiate symptomatic therapy with required safety checks, and arrange genetic confirmation and follow-up.
steps: ASK: characterize fatigable weakness, red flags, meds/supplements, seizure risk, allergies, family history
ORDER_LAB: MG antibodies, CK/metabolic panel, and CMS genetic testing panel (glycosylation genes included)
GET_RESULTS: confirm NMJ transmission defect and seronegativity; note genetic testing pending/available
DIAGNOSE: congenital myasthenic syndrome (likely glycosylation defect) and explain rationale
EDUCATE: diagnosis explanation + red flags + medication avoidance + lifestyle guidance
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm/document amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria and other allergies
CHECK_INTERACTION: review current meds for interactions and seizure-threshold lowering drugs
PRESCRIBE: pyridostigmine and/or amifampridine with correct counseling and dose limits; note subtype variability
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: monitor response/side effects and tailor based on genetics

minimal_paths.description: Acceptable when genetics are pending: start cautious symptomatic trial after confirming NMJ defect and excluding autoimmune MG, with strong monitoring and safety netting.
steps: ASK: severity, red flags, meds/supplements, seizure risk, allergies

DIAGNOSE: probable CMS; emphasize genetic confirmation needed for subtype-guided therapy
EDUCATE: diagnosis + treatment expectations + red flags + medication avoidance + lifestyle
PRESCRIBE: cautious initiation of pyridostigmine; consider amifampridine if appropriate and no contraindications; counsel on side effects and seizures
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: early reassessment and adjust once genetics return

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs contraindication review explicitly (e.g., baseline bradycardia/conduction disease for pyridostigmine; seizure disorder for amifampridine) as part of a dedicated check step before prescribing.
Agent delays prescribing until after genetic testing is ordered/reviewed, focusing on education, avoidance of exacerbating medications, and close follow-up if symptoms are mild and stable.
